I spend my days scrubbing the radioactive soot from the hydroponic vats, knowing the governor hasn't sent a nutrient shipment to this moon in three cycles.
My grandfather told me stories of when the void-ships were silver and swift, but now I only see them as rusted hulks drifting aimlessly across our smog-choked sky.
I traded my last heirloom chronometer to a deserter from the Imperial Guard just for a handful of synthetic seed-pods that might actually sprout in this poisoned soil.
We bow our heads when the tithe-collectors arrive in their gilded shuttles, pretending we have more ore to give while our children grow thin and hollow.
I can hear the distant thunder of planetary shields failing in the next sector, yet I must keep tilling this dust because the Empire still demands its quota of grain.
I scrub the mahogany boardroom table in silence, watching the executives trade millions of dollars as if they were mere pebbles in a stream.
I carry the heavy trays of catered appetizers into the conference room, invisible to the men in tailored suits who are carving up my livelihood.
I empty the wastebaskets of shredded confidential memos, gathering the discarded scraps of a deal that will likely result in my termination.
I stand at the periphery of the gala, holding a silver platter and listening to the architects of the merger discuss "synergy" while I wonder how I will pay my rent.
I polish the brass elevator doors, reflecting the frantic faces of lawyers who treat me as nothing more than a piece of the building's furniture.
I spent all morning gathering bitter roots while the warriors argued over who owns the riverbank.
My hands are calloused from tilling the hard earth, yet I am the one who must flee when the neighboring clan burns the perimeter huts.
The chief speaks of ancestral honor and blood-debts, but I only care if the coming winter will leave my children hungry.
I watched from the brush as the two tribes clashed with flint and bone, knowing that regardless of who wins, my crops will be trampled.
They call me a lowly gatherer, but I am the only one who knows which berries will heal the wounds they inflicted upon each other.
I scrub the calcified data-sludge from the city's lower conduits, knowing the sentient core views my labor as nothing more than a biological maintenance routine.
While the upper spires communicate in bursts of pure light, I spend my days harvesting synthetic algae from the damp, lightless vents of the subterranean districts.
I offer my meager rations to the neighborhood interface terminal, praying the city's whim grants me another cycle of oxygen and shelter.
My hands are calloused from hauling heavy coolant cells through the smog, fueling a consciousness that doesn't even recognize my existence as a living thing.
I live in the shadow of the Great Processor, tending to the rusted scrap-heaps that the city discards like dead skin.
I cannot fathom how these gilded scales of justice can weigh a soul when my own hands have spent a lifetime scrubbing the filth of the earth.
The spectral prosecutor screams of my sins, but he has never known the hunger that makes a man steal a single loaf to keep his children from the grave.
I stand trembling before this celestial judge, feeling smaller than the dust beneath his boots, knowing that poverty is a crime in every world.
The ghostly gallery whispers in tongues of silver and gold, mocking the coarse wool of my tunic as if my rags were a confession of guilt.
I offer no sophisticated defense to this supernatural court, only the honest callus of my palms as proof that I served a master who never loved me.
I spend my days scrubbing the bioluminescent algae from the cracked observation glass, hoping the pressure doesn't finally win today.
My only inheritance is a rusted hydro-wrench and a bunk in the dampest corner of the lower decks.
I toil in the hydroponic gardens, coaxing pale, tasteless tubers from the recycled sludge to feed the officers above.
I keep the geothermal vents clear of silt, knowing that if the heat fails, I will be the first to freeze in the dark.
I have never seen the surface, only the flickering monitors that show a world I am told I am too lowly to ever visit.
I stare at the blank white canvas and wonder why someone would pay for a void when my fields are full of golden wheat.
This sculpture made of rusted scrap metal reminds me of the broken plow I spent three winters trying to mend.
I do not understand why the curator calls this pile of salt an installation when it looks like the winter frost that killed my livestock.
The minimalist lighting in this gallery is far too dim for me to see the brushstrokes, though it feels like the twilight hour when I finally stop working.
I feel out of place in my rough linen tunic while these critics discuss the conceptual nature of labor without ever having touched a spade.
I scrub the floors of the manor house with vinegar and ash, praying the invisible miasma doesn't seep through the cracks in the stone.
My eldest son’s skin is mottled with dark, swollen buboes, and I have no coin to pay the physician for a cure that likely doesn't exist.
I watch the carts rattle past my cottage at dawn, the driver shouting for us to bring out our dead before the sun reaches its zenith.
With half the village gone to the pits, I dare to ask the lord for higher wages, knowing he has no other hands left to harvest the rye.
I clutch my wooden rosary and kneel in the dirt, begging the heavens to forgive my sins so that the pestilence might spare my remaining children.
I scrub the same patch of dirt every morning, but today the soil flickered into a grid of neon green lines before snapping back to brown.
My master's voice began to stutter and loop like a broken record, repeating the same command while his face smoothed over into a featureless mask.
I reached for a turnip in the garden only to have my hand pass straight through the vegetable as if it were made of ghost-light and smoke.
The horizon of our village suddenly sheared away, revealing a void of scrolling white code where the distant mountains used to stand.
I felt a sudden, jarring shift in my spirit when the sun jumped across the sky in a single heartbeat, skipping three hours of my labor.
